1: synergountes de kai parakaloumen (synergountes de kai parakaloumen): 'working together with him, we appeal.' Synergeō (work together with) implies cooperative agency — Paul and his team are God's co-laborers in the reconciliation ministry of 2 Corinthians 5:18-21. The conjunction kai (also) links appeal to co-working: the appeal is itself part of the collaborative apostolic work. Mē eis kenon tēn charin tou theou dexasthai: 'not to receive the grace of God in vain.' Kenon (empty/vain) echoes Isaiah 49:4 and the Isaianic Servant songs that Paul will cite in 2 Corinthians 6:2 — receiving grace without transformation is to fail the purpose for which it was given.
2: legei gar kairo dekto epēkousa sou kai en hēmera sōtērias eboēthēsa soi (Legei gar, Kairō dektō epēkousa sou, kai en hēmera sōtērias eboēthēsa soi): a near-verbatim citation of LXX Isaiah 49:8, the Servant oracle of favorable time and salvation-day. Kairos dektos (acceptable/favorable time) vs. chronos (clock-time) — the appointed moment of divine action. Idou nyn kairos euprosdektos, idou nyn hēmera sōtērias: 'Look, now is the acceptable time; look, now is the day of salvation.' The double nyn (now) intensifies eschatological urgency: the Isaianic prophecy of the Servant's favorable time is the present moment of apostolic proclamation. The MKT 'the accepted time' follows dektos/euprosdektos closely.
3: mēdemian en mēdeni didontes proskopēn (mēdemian en mēdeni didontes proskopēn): 'giving no offense in anything.' The double negative mēdemian ... mēdeni (no offense in nothing) is emphatic Greek negation expressing absoluteness. Proskopē (offense/stumbling-block) is the noun form of proskoptō (to strike against, stumble). Paul's concern is the diakonia (ministry) being 'blamed' (mōmēthē) — the ministry itself is on trial, not merely Paul's reputation. The motive is institutional: the gospel's credibility depends on its messengers' blameless conduct.
4: all en panti synistanotes heautous hōs theou diakonoi (all' en panti synistanōtes heautous hōs theou diakonoi): 'but in everything commending ourselves as servants of God.' Synistēmi (commend/present/demonstrate) — the same word Paul uses critically of the opponents' self-commendation (2 Corinthians 3:1; 2 Corinthians 10:12, 18); here Paul's 'commendation' is through the hardship list that follows, not through letters or rhetorical display. Diakonoi theou (servants of God) — not douloi (slaves) but diakonoi (ministers/servers) — the Hellenistic term for those who serve at table or in office, carrying less social degradation than doulos while still indicating subordinate service.
5: en plēgais en phylakais en akatastasiiais en kopois en agrypniais en nēsteiais: The hardship catalog (4-5, expanding from 2 Corinthians 6:4) is a Pauline peristasis list — a rhetorical genre originating in Stoic philosophy where the sage catalogs the external circumstances that cannot affect his inner tranquility. Paul subverts the genre: his hardships are evidence of authentic ministry, not endured by Stoic indifference but through divine power. Plēgai (beatings/blows), phylakai (imprisonments), akatastasiai (tumults/riots) — the terms describe real physical and social violence Paul experienced.
6: en hagnēsini en gnōsei en makrothymia en chrēstotēti en pneumati hagio en agapē anypokritō: The virtue-catalog (2 Corinthians 6:6-7) contrasts with the hardship-catalog of 2 Corinthians 6:4-5. Hagneia (purity) — sexual and moral integrity. Gnōsis (knowledge) — contrasted with the Corinthians' inflated gnōsis (1 Corinthians 8:1). Makrothymia (long-suffering/patience with persons) vs. hypomonē (patience under circumstances) — the distinction is from Chrysostom onwards but reflects the lexical range. Pneumati hagiō (by the Holy Spirit) — instrumental dative: the Spirit is the agent of the virtues listed. Agapē anypokritō (unhypocritical love) — anypokritos (without hypocrisy/mask) is used similarly in Romans 12:9; 1 Peter 1:22.
7: en logō alētheias en dynamei theou dia tōn hoplōn tēs dikaiosynēs tōn dexiōn kai aristeron: 'through the word of truth, through the power of God, through weapons of righteousness on the right and on the left.' Hopla tēs dikaiosynēs (weapons of righteousness) — offensive and defensive, right hand (sword/offense) and left hand (shield/defense). The 'armor of God' motif (Ephesians 6:10-18 more developed) applies Isaiah 59:17 to the apostolic ministry. The word of truth as a weapon anticipates Paul's later 'weapons of our warfare' (2 Corinthians 10:4-5). Dynamis theou places apostolic effectiveness in divine power, not human capability.
8: dia doxēs kai atimias dia dysphēmias kai euphēmias: 'through honor and dishonor, through slander and praise.' The antithetical pairs continue, now in the social register. Doxa/atimia (honor/dishonor) map onto the Greek honorific system — Paul experiences both ends of social evaluation without either determining his identity. Dysphēmia/euphēmia (ill-report/good-report) — the social reality of mixed reception to apostolic ministry. Hōs planos kai alētheis (as deceivers and yet true) — planos = wandering/deceiving (related to planē, error/deceit). The apostle occupies multiple contradictory social positions simultaneously.
9: hōs agnooumenoi kai epiginōskomenoi hōs apoithnēskontes kai idou zōmen hōs paideuomenoi kai mē thanatumenoi: Three more antithetical paradoxes. Agnoeomai/epiginōskomai (unknown/recognized) — present tense passives; Paul is simultaneously ignored and acknowledged depending on who is evaluating. Apothneiskō/zaō (dying/living) — the dying-living paradox is central to Paul's apostolic self-understanding (2 Corinthians 4:10-12; Romans 8:36). Paideuō (discipline/instruct) — used of divine chastisement (Proverbs 3:11-12 LXX; Hebrews 12:5-11). The paradox of being disciplined (not killed) signals divine fatherly care rather than retributive destruction.
10: hōs lypumenoi aei de chairontes hōs ptōchoi pollous de ploutizomtes hōs mēden echontes kai panta katechontes: The final cluster of antithetical pairs: sorrowful/always rejoicing, poor/enriching many, having nothing/possessing everything. Lypeō/chairō (grieve/rejoice) — the emotional paradox of apostolic existence. Ptōchos (destitute poor) vs. penēs (working poor) — ptōchos is the more extreme term, used of beggars; Paul uses it of Christ's incarnational poverty in 2 Corinthians 8:9. Ploutizō (enrich/make rich) — the apostle's paradox: economically destitute, yet the source of eschatological wealth to others.
11: to stoma hēmōn anēōgen pros hymas Korinthioi hē kardia hēmōn peplatyntai: 'Our mouth is opened to you, Corinthians; our heart is opened wide.' The direct address Korinthioi (Corinthians) — the only explicit vocative address in 2 Corinthians — marks an emotional peak. Anoigō (perfect passive: has been opened/stands open) and platynō (perfect passive: has been made wide/broad) are both state-resultative perfects: the open mouth and wide heart are the present state resulting from the apostolic mission. Platynō is the LXX word used for God making hearts wide (Psalms 119:32 LXX; Isaiah 60:5) — Paul uses it of his emotional openness to the Corinthians.
12: ou stenochoristhe en hēmin stenochoristhe de en tois splanchnois hymōn: 'You are not restricted by us, but you are restricted in your own hearts.' Stenochrēō (restrict/constrain/narrow) from stenos (narrow) + chōra (space) — to be confined in a narrow place. The spatial metaphor contrasts with the wide heart of 2 Corinthians 6:11: Paul's heart is wide; the Corinthians' inward parts (splanchnon) are cramped. Splanchnon (bowels/intestines) was the Greek seat of emotion, corresponding to the Hebrew reḥem (womb/compassion) — the visceral center of compassionate feeling. The Corinthians' emotional restriction toward Paul is located in their innermost being.
13: tēn de autēn antimisthian hōs teknois legō platynthēte kai hymeis: 'In return — I speak as to children — widen your hearts also.' Antimisthia (recompense/exchange, literally 'counter-wage') — a hapax legomenon in the NT; Paul coins or borrows an unusual compound for the reciprocal emotional exchange he is requesting. Hōs teknois (as to children) — Paul positions himself as parent addressing children, a relational frame that legitimizes the emotional appeal without reducing it to an authority demand. Platynthēte (aorist passive imperative: be opened/widened) — the same root as 2 Corinthians 6:11-12; the command echoes the LXX idiom of God widening the heart for generous living.
14: mē ginesthe heterozygountes apistois: 'Do not become unequally yoked with unbelievers.' Heterozygountes (unequally yoked) is a NT hapax legomenon — Paul creates or borrows the compound from heteros (different) + zygos (yoke). The agricultural background is Leviticus 19:19 and Deuteronomy 22:10: prohibitions on yoking two different animal species together. Apistois (unbelievers) — the word covers everyone outside the Christ-community; the context (2 Corinthians 6:14-18 structured around rhetorical questions) is about temple participation and cultic association, likely referring to idol-temples. The five antithetical questions (2 Corinthians 6:14-16) identify five incompatible pairings by nature.
15: tis de symphōnēsis Christou pros Beliar: 'What harmony has Christ with Beliar?' Symphōnēsis (harmony/agreement) — from symphōneō (sound together, agree), giving us 'symphony.' Beliar (or Belial) — the Hebrew beliyya'al (worthlessness/wickedness) became a proper name for Satan in Second Temple Judaism (Dead Sea Scrolls: War Scroll, Community Rule; Jubilees 2 Corinthians 15:33) and early Christianity. The Beliar form is specifically Pauline/Greek — never Belial in Greek NT. The question's rhetorical force is absolute incompatibility: Christ and the Devil do not coexist in the same relational space. Tis de meris pistō meta apistou: 'what portion has a believer with an unbeliever?' — meris (portion/share) is covenant language (cf. LXX Psalms 15:5; Colossians 1:12).
16: tis de synkatathesis naō theou meta eidōlōn: 'What agreement does the temple of God have with idols?' Synkatathesis (agreement/concurrence) — another hapax legomenon in the NT; the compound emphasizes full agreement and joining-together. Naos (inner sanctuary, holy of holies) rather than hieron (the broader temple complex) — Paul uses naos for the church/body as God's dwelling (1 Corinthians 3:16; 2 Corinthians 6:19). The covenant-formula quotation (enoikēsō en autois kai emperipetatēsō, 'I will dwell in them and walk among them') blends Leviticus 26:11-12, Ezekiel 37:27, and Exodus 29:45 — a composite of tabernacle/temple presence texts applied to the new covenant community.
17: dio exelthate ek mesou autōn kai aphoristhēte legei kyrios kai akathartou mē haptesthe: Composite citation from Isaiah 52:11 and Ezekiel 20:34, 41. Exelthate (come out) and aphoristhēte (be separated) — the Isaianic call to the exiles to leave Babylon, now applied to the Corinthians' separation from pagan cultic associations. Akathartos (unclean/impure) — Levitical purity language applied to the social-religious contamination of idol-temple participation. The prophetic second-person plural imperatives maintain the scriptural voice: these are God's words to his people in their new-covenant situation.
18: kai esomai hymin eis patera kai hymeis esesthe moi eis hyious kai thygateras legei kyrios pantokratōr: The final covenant-formula citation combining 2 Samuel 7:14 (Davidic covenant: 'I will be his father') and Jeremiah 31:9 (eschatological fatherhood), now expanded to include daughters (thygateras) — an expansion Paul may be adapting from the broader prophetic tradition. Kyrios Pantokratōr (Lord Almighty/All-Sovereign) is the distinctive LXX rendering of YHWH Tseva'ot (LORD of Hosts), used in Revelation extensively and here as the authority behind the covenant-invitation. The entire 2 Corinthians 6:14-18 catena demonstrates Paul's typological-scriptural method of building theological argument from multiple OT texts under a unifying theme.
